data.q: What is the primary architectural difference between 'Signals' and traditional 'Store-based' state (like Redux)?
o: Signals only work in the browser, while Redux works on the server
Signals use fine-grained reactivity to update only the specific parts of the UI that depend on a value
Signals require a central dispatcher to manage all actions
There is no functional difference; they are different names for the same thing
data.e: Unlike Redux, which often triggers a re-render of large component trees, signals track dependencies at a granular level, updating only the specific DOM nodes or components that 'consume' the signal.

data.q: What are the three core primitives typically found in a Signal-based system?
o: State, Action, Reducer
Signal (Source), Computed (Derived), and Effect (Sink)
Get, Set, Delete
Observable, Subscriber, Operator
data.e: Signals hold values, Computed signals derive new values from other signals, and Effects perform side-effects (like DOM updates or logging) when dependencies change.

data.q: What is 'Glitch-Free' reactivity in the context of Signals?
o: A guarantee that the UI will never flicker
Ensuring that derived state never exposes an inconsistent intermediate value during a synchronous update
Automatically fixing syntax errors in the code
A method for preventing network errors
data.e: In a 'glitchy' system, a derived value might update multiple times if its dependencies change simultaneously. Signals use a push/pull or topological sort mechanism to ensure the final value is computed only once.

o: The developer must manually list all dependencies in an array
They use automatic subscription tracking by intercepting property access during execution
They scan the entire file system for variable names
They rely on the file import structure
data.e: When a signal is read inside a computed function or effect, it registers itself as a dependency automatically, removing the need for 'dependency arrays' like those in React's `useEffect`.

data.q: What is the 'Pull' aspect of the 'Push/Pull' hybrid model used by modern Signal libraries?
o: Pulling data from a remote API
Lazily evaluating computed signals only when they are actually read
Downloading the library from a CDN
Getting a user's input from a form
data.e: To optimize performance, signals often 'push' a notification that something has changed, but only 'pull' (calculate) the actual new value when it is needed by the UI.

data.q: Which framework is largely credited with the modern resurgence of Signals in the web ecosystem?
o: React
SolidJS
Next.js
jQuery
data.e: SolidJS pioneered the use of signals for a 'no-VDOM' approach, proving that fine-grained reactivity could lead to industry-leading performance benchmarks.

data.q: What happens when a Signal's value is updated but no parts of the UI are currently using it?
o: The whole application crashes
Nothing happens; no computations or DOM updates are triggered (Laziness)
The server is notified of the unused state
The signal is automatically deleted from memory
data.e: Signals are inherently efficient because they only perform work when there is a 'subscriber' (like a visible UI component) interested in the result.

data.q: In a Signal-based UI, how are DOM updates typically performed?
o: The framework re-runs the entire component function
Directly targeting and updating the specific DOM node's property without a Virtual DOM diffing step
By refreshing the entire page
By sending the updated state to the server first
data.e: By bypassing the Virtual DOM, Signal-based frameworks can achieve near-native performance, as they know exactly which element needs to change at the moment the signal updates.

o: A signal that can only be read by the server
A signal that allows both reading and direct updating (setting) of its value
A signal used for saving data to a file
A signal that is written in TypeScript
data.e: Writable signals are the 'sources of truth' in your application state, while computed signals are read-only views of that state.

data.q: What is the benefit of 'Automatic Cleanup' in Effects?
o: It deletes old comments from the code
It automatically unsubscribes and cleans up resources (like timers or event listeners) when the effect is re-run or disposed
It clears the browser's cache
It formats the code according to Prettier rules
data.e: Cleanup ensures that your application doesn't leak memory or leave 'ghost' event listeners active as users navigate through different views.

data.q: How does the 'Preact Signals' implementation differ from React's `useState`?
o: It can only store numbers
It can be used outside of components and doesn't trigger a component re-render unless explicitly used in the JSX
It requires a 100MB download
It only works with classes
data.e: Preact signals can live in global scope and allow the UI to 'tunnel' updates directly to the DOM, often avoiding the heavy work of the React-style reconciliation process.

o: Comparing two different signal libraries
Preventing updates from propagating if the new value is identical to the old value
Checking if the user's password is correct
Ensuring all variables have the same name
data.e: If you set a signal to its current value, the system detects no change and halts the reactive chain early, preventing unnecessary computations or UI updates.
